segments.title: How Sanjusangen-do preserves contemplation amid mass tourism
segments.summary: A visit to Sanjusangen-do considers how a crowded urban heritage site can retain the quiet and contemplative character of a temple.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses Sanjusangen-do to distinguish encountering cultural heritage from simply collecting facts and figures. Although the coaches and crowds initially make the Kyoto temple look like a tourist trap, he finds the interior quiet enough for contemplation despite its busy urban setting. He credits the way large groups disperse through the site, allowing individual visitors space rather than overwhelming the experience. The observation concerns present-day heritage practice rather than the temple's historical chronology: the learning point is how a heavily visited religious site can retain its spiritual character while serving mass tourism. His closing comparison with other Japanese temples leads him to see deliberate care for these spaces as a major part of their appeal.

evidence.note: He contrasts an initial impression of a tourist trap with the quiet, visitor flow, and contemplative experience preserved inside the temple grounds.
